verses.commentary: I ask then: Did God reject His people? By no means! I am an Israelite myself, a descendant of Abraham, from the tribe of Benjamin. Paul's question addresses a natural conclusion from chapters 9-10. God has not rejected Israel. Paul himself is proof: an Israelite, a believer in Christ. God's promises to Israel still stand.

God did not reject His people, whom He foreknew. Don't you know what Scripture says in the passage about Elijah, how he appealed to God against Israel. Foreknew implies electing love, not mere prescience. God knew and chose Israel. The Elijah example will illustrate the remnant principle.

Lord, they have killed your prophets and torn down your altars; I am the only one left, and they are trying to kill me (1 Kings 19:10, 14)? Elijah's despair: I alone am left. He thought faithfulness had disappeared. This is the cry of a discouraged prophet who sees only apostasy.

And what was God's answer to him? I have reserved for myself seven thousand who have not bowed the knee to Baal (1 Kings 19:18). God's response: You are not alone. Seven thousand remained faithful. God always preserves a remnant. Faithfulness is never as rare as it seems. God keeps His own.

So too, at the present time there is a remnant chosen by grace. The remnant principle applies in Paul's day. A remnant of Israel believes in Christ. Chosen by grace emphasizes divine election, not human merit. Grace, not ethnicity, defines the remnant.

And if by grace, then it cannot be based on works; if it were, grace would no longer be grace. Grace and works are mutually exclusive. Adding works to grace destroys grace. Salvation is entirely by grace or not at all. This is a foundational principle.

What then? What the people of Israel sought so earnestly they did not obtain. The elect among them did, but the others were hardened. Israel sought righteousness but did not find it, except for the elect remnant. The others were hardened (pōroō, πωρόω, made dull, insensitive). God's judicial hardening is a response to persistent unbelief.

As it is written: God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that could not see and ears that could not hear, to this very day (Deuteronomy 29:4, Isaiah 29:10). Judicial blindness: God gave them spiritual stupor. They cannot see or hear spiritually. This is God's judgment on unbelief. To this very day shows it continues.

And David says: May their table become a snare and a trap, a stumbling block and a retribution for them (Psalm 69:22). David's imprecation is applied to Israel. What should bless (the table) becomes a curse. Blessing rejected becomes judgment. This is the irony of hardening.

May their eyes be darkened so they cannot see, and their backs be bent forever (Psalm 69:23). Darkened eyes and bent backs depict spiritual blindness and burden. Those who reject light are given over to darkness. This is both judgment and mercy withheld.

Again I ask: Did they stumble so as to fall beyond recovery? Not at all! Rather, because of their transgression, salvation has come to the Gentiles to make Israel envious. Israel stumbled but did not fall beyond recovery. Their rejection opened the door for Gentile salvation. God's purpose: to provoke Israel to jealousy, moving them to embrace Christ. Israel's fall is not final.

But if their transgression means riches for the world, and their loss means riches for the Gentiles, how much greater riches will their full inclusion bring! From lesser to greater: Israel's rejection brought blessing to Gentiles. Israel's full acceptance will bring even greater blessing. Their full inclusion (plērōma, πλήρωμα, fullness) suggests future restoration.

I am talking to you Gentiles. Inasmuch as I am the apostle to the Gentiles, I take pride in my ministry. Paul addresses Gentiles directly. His role as apostle to the Gentiles is a source of pride (not arrogance but joy in calling). His Gentile mission serves a larger purpose.

In the hope that I may somehow arouse my own people to envy and save some of them. Paul's strategy: make Israel envious through Gentile blessing. If Israel sees Gentiles enjoying covenant blessings, they may seek Christ. Save some acknowledges not all will respond, but some will.

For if their rejection brought reconciliation to the world, what will their acceptance be but life from the dead? Another greater-to-lesser argument. Rejection brought reconciliation (the gospel went to Gentiles). Acceptance will bring life from the dead. This likely refers to resurrection and the age to come. Israel's restoration signals the end times.

If the part of the dough offered as firstfruits is holy, then the whole batch is holy; if the root is holy, so are the branches. Two metaphors: firstfruits and root. Firstfruits (patriarchs) sanctify the whole (Israel). Root (Abraham and the patriarchs) sanctifies branches (descendants). Israel retains a holy status because of God's covenant with the fathers.

If some of the branches have been broken off, and you, though a wild olive shoot, have been grafted in among the others and now share in the nourishing sap from the olive root. The olive tree is Israel/the people of God. Some natural branches (unbelieving Jews) were broken off. Wild olive shoots (Gentiles) were grafted in. Gentiles now share in covenant blessings (the nourishing sap).

Do not consider yourself to be superior to those other branches. If you do, consider this: You do not support the root, but the root supports you. Warning against Gentile pride. Gentiles are grafted in; they do not replace Israel. The root (patriarchs, covenant) supports the branches, not vice versa. Gentiles owe everything to Israel's heritage.

You will say then, Branches were broken off so that I could be grafted in. A potential boast: Israel was cut off to make room for me. This misunderstands God's purposes. Israel's rejection was not for Gentile superiority but for global salvation.

Granted. But they were broken off because of unbelief, and you stand by faith. Do not be arrogant, but tremble. Yes, branches were broken off, but the reason was unbelief. Gentiles stand by faith, not superior merit. Arrogance is dangerous. Tremble (phobou, φοβοῦ, fear) is a call to humble reverence. Pride precedes a fall.

For if God did not spare the natural branches, He will not spare you either. If God judged unbelieving Israel, He will judge unbelieving Gentiles. Natural branches (ethnic Israel) were not exempt. Wild branches (Gentiles) will not be either. God's justice is impartial.

Consider therefore the kindness and sternness of God: sternness to those who fell, but kindness to you, provided that you continue in His kindness. Otherwise, you also will be cut off. God's character includes kindness (chrēstotēs, χρηστότης, goodness) and sternness (apotomia, ἀποτομία, severity). Sternness fell on unbelieving Israel. Kindness is shown to believing Gentiles, but only if they continue in faith. Apostasy results in being cut off. This is a warning against presumption.

And if they do not persist in unbelief, they will be grafted in, for God is able to graft them in again. Israel's restoration is possible. If they abandon unbelief, they will be re-grafted. God is able emphasizes divine power. What seems impossible (restoring Israel) is possible with God.

After all, if you were cut out of an olive tree that is wild by nature, and contrary to nature were grafted into a cultivated olive tree, how much more readily will these, the natural branches, be grafted into their own olive tree! Grafting Gentiles (wild) into Israel's tree was contrary to nature. Re-grafting Jews (natural) is according to nature. If God did the harder thing (grafting in Gentiles), He will certainly do the easier thing (restoring Israel). Israel's restoration is more natural than Gentile inclusion.

I do not want you to be ignorant of this mystery, brothers and sisters, so that you may not be conceited: Israel has experienced a hardening in part until the full number of the Gentiles has come in. A mystery (mystērion, μυστήριον): a truth once hidden, now revealed. Israel's hardening is partial (not all) and temporary (until). The full number of Gentiles refers to the completion of Gentile salvation. Purpose: to prevent Gentile conceit. Gentiles should not despise Israel; God's plan includes both.

And in this way all Israel will be saved. As it is written: The deliverer will come from Zion; He will turn godlessness away from Jacob (Isaiah 59:20-21). All Israel does not mean every individual Jew but the nation corporately, or the full number of elect Jews. In this way suggests a process: after Gentile fullness, Israel's salvation. The Deliverer is Christ. He will return and turn Israel from godlessness. This is future, eschatological.

And this is my covenant with them when I take away their sins (Isaiah 27:9, Jeremiah 31:33-34). God's covenant promise: sins will be removed. This is the new covenant. Israel's future includes full forgiveness and restoration. God's covenant faithfulness ensures it.

As far as the gospel is concerned, they are enemies for your sake; but as far as election is concerned, they are loved on account of the patriarchs. Two perspectives on Israel: enemies regarding the gospel (their rejection opened the door for Gentiles); loved regarding election (God's choice of the patriarchs). Israel is both judged and loved. God's election is irrevocable.

For God's gifts and His call are irrevocable. Irrevocable (ametamelētos, ἀμεταμέλητος) means without repentance, unchangeable. God does not revoke His gifts (covenant blessings) or call (election of Israel). This guarantees Israel's future restoration. God's promises stand forever.

Just as you who were at one time disobedient to God have now received mercy as a result of their disobedience. Gentiles were disobedient but received mercy through Israel's disobedience. Israel's rejection became the occasion for Gentile salvation. God's sovereignty orchestrates even rebellion for merciful purposes.

So they too have now become disobedient in order that they too may now receive mercy as a result of God's mercy to you. Israel's disobedience leads to their eventual reception of mercy. God's mercy to Gentiles provokes Israel to jealousy, leading to their salvation. Mercy begets mercy. God's plan encompasses all.

For God has bound everyone over to disobedience so that He may have mercy on them all. Universal disobedience (Jew and Gentile) reveals universal need for mercy. God's purpose: to have mercy on all (both groups). All are guilty so all must receive mercy. This magnifies grace. Salvation is entirely of mercy, not merit.

Oh, the depth of the riches of the wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable His judgments, and His paths beyond tracing out! A doxology of wonder. God's riches, wisdom, and knowledge are unfathomable. His judgments (krisis, κρίσις, decisions) are unsearchable. His paths (hodos, ὁδός, ways) are untraceable. We cannot fully comprehend God's plans. Humble awe is the right response.

Who has known the mind of the Lord? Or who has been His counselor? (Isaiah 40:13). Rhetorical questions expecting no. No one knows God's mind fully. No one advises God. He needs no counsel. This underscores divine sovereignty and human finitude.

Who has ever given to God, that God should repay them? (Job 41:11). No one can put God in debt. God owes nothing to anyone. All good comes from His grace, not our merit. We are recipients, not creditors.

For from Him and through Him and for Him are all things. To Him be the glory forever! Amen. The climax: God is the source (from Him), sustainer (through Him), and goal (for Him) of all things. All things exist because of God, are sustained by God, and exist for God's glory. To Him be the glory forever captures the purpose of all existence. Amen (so be it) seals the doxology.
